findings.message: `cargo make track-branch-create` already materializes `metadata.json.branch` and persists the activation artifacts, so the command's next step to edit `metadata.json.branch` again leaves a fresh `updated_at` change after the activation commit instead of the clean post-init state the workflow expects. This step should be a verification/reporting step, or the wrapper behavior must be changed with the command updated accordingly.

The behavior section still describes looping into a state machine and the Phase 0 -> Phase 1 transition ownership. The harness policy for `/track:init` explicitly excludes state-machine transition/back-and-forth loop wording from phase commands, so this leaks orchestration policy into the standalone Phase 0 command.

The execution sequence no longer matches the required five-step `/track:init` flow: ADR pre-check is supposed to be step 1, followed by metadata creation, branch creation, metadata branch update, and verification. This command inserts track-id derivation as step 1 and shifts ADR pre-check to step 2, producing a six-step sequence that violates the harness-policy briefing and can cause downstream reviewers/tools to treat the command contract as non-compliant.

The briefing explicitly excludes state-machine transition and back-and-forth loop wording from this command, but this behavior section still mentions looping into a state machine and the `/track:plan` Phase 0 -> Phase 1 transition. Even though it says `/track:init` does not own that logic, including the excluded transition/loop language makes the command policy non-compliant for this scope.

`cargo make track-branch-create` already materializes `metadata.json.branch` and persists the activation artifacts before switching branches, so step 4 edits `metadata.json.branch` and refreshes `updated_at` a second time after the activation commit. That leaves the newly initialized track dirty and out of sync with the wrapper's committed activation state; this step should verify/report the branch value or the wrapper behavior must be changed to match the documented five-step flow.

The wrapper still performs local pre-check orchestration (`spec.json` existence and `architecture-rules.json` validation) before invoking `type-designer`. Round 3 explicitly narrowed this command to subagent invocation plus result receipt only, so these checks reintroduce workflow logic outside the subagent and break the intended minimal wrapper shape.

`.claude/commands/track/type-design.md` still tells the subagent to accept an optional `--layer` filter. That reintroduces the argument-handling the briefing explicitly removed and conflicts with the new always-process-all-layers contract for Phase 2.

Deleting `.claude/commands/track/design.md` and replacing it with `type-design.md` changes the slash-command name to `/track:type-design`, but the rest of the workflow still invokes and documents `/track:design`. That leaves the documented Phase 2 entry point unreachable unless a compatibility alias is kept.

Deleting `.claude/commands/track/design.md` and replacing it with `.claude/commands/track/type-design.md` changes the slash-command name, but the workflow still has live `/track:design` call sites. Without a compatibility alias or a repo-wide update in the same change, Phase 2 becomes unreachable from those existing entry points.

The diff deletes the tracked `.claude/commands/track/design.md` and updates the orchestrator to call `/track:type-design`, but `.claude/commands/track/type-design.md` is untracked and therefore not part of the patch shown by `git diff`. If this is committed as-is, Phase 2 will point at a command file that is not added to the repository, breaking the planning workflow for type-design.

`spec-designer` is now instructed to run `bin/sotp track views sync`, but that command also rewrites `plan.md` in addition to `spec.md` (`sync_rendered_views` does both on the single-track path). That lets a Phase 1 agent mutate a file its own scope explicitly forbids and can interfere with Phase 3 ownership. Use a spec-only render path here, or explicitly narrow the command so it cannot touch `plan.md`.

`spec-designer` directs the agent to regenerate `spec.md` with `bin/sotp track views sync`, but that command only syncs `plan.md` and `registry.md`. As written, Phase 1 will not produce the required `spec.md` view and may clobber the wrong rendered files instead.

`type-designer` names the rendered artifact `<layer>-type-signals.md`, but the current `type-signals` renderer emits `<layer>-types.md` and only writes `<layer>-type-signals.json` for the evaluation result. This points the phase-2 pipeline at a non-existent markdown artifact.

This instruction routes Phase 1 through `bin/sotp track views sync` and `bin/sotp verify plan-artifact-refs`, but `track views sync` also syncs `plan.md` and `registry.md` (`libs/infrastructure/src/track/render.rs:1`) and the repo's spec-signal path is `sotp track signals` (`libs/domain/src/spec.rs:376`, `apps/cli/src/commands/track/signals.rs:64`). A spec-designer following this text can overwrite files outside its ownership and run the wrong gate.

`type-signals` does not produce `<layer>-type-signals.md`; it writes `<layer>-types.md` plus `<layer>-type-signals.json`. The phase-2 contract is pointing at the wrong artifact name, so a literal subagent would manage a file the CLI never emits.

The permitted render outputs list only `<layer>-graph.md`, but `bin/sotp track type-graph` defaults to cluster mode (`--cluster-depth 2`) and writes a `<layer>-graph/` directory with `index.md` plus cluster files. The scope rule does not cover the command's actual default output.

`spec.md` is described as a generated artifact produced by `bin/sotp track signals`, but the generic `Write`/`Edit` allowance still covers it. That lets the agent bypass the renderer and manually edit a derived view, risking drift from `spec.json` and the signal counts.

The scope explicitly allows direct writes to baseline and rendered-view artifacts, even though the internal pipeline says those files are produced by `bin/sotp track baseline-capture`, `type-graph`, `contract-map`, and `type-signals`. That opens the door to hand-editing derived artifacts outside the canonical generation path, which can leave the views inconsistent with `<layer>-types.json`.

The Phase 2 pipeline tells the subagent to render the contract map with `bin/sotp track contract-map <id> [--layer <layer_id>]`, but the actual `contract-map` CLI uses the plural `--layers` option for layer filtering. A type-designer following this contract will run an invalid command and fail the required render/gate step for layer-scoped `/track:type-design` runs; change this invocation to `--layers <layer_id>` or omit the filter when rendering all layers.

The Phase 2 internal pipeline tells the subagent to run `bin/sotp track contract-map <id> [--layer <layer_id>]`, but the actual `contract-map` command exposes `--layers`, not `--layer`, and writes one shared `contract-map.md`. Following this contract will make the type-designer fail during the required view-render step, so `/track:type-design` cannot reliably complete its direct-writer pipeline. Use the supported `--layers` form or render the shared contract map once after all scoped layer catalogues are written.

AC-19 uses `BranchMode::Create / resume flow` as the negated condition, but the rest of the track defines the relaxed preflight as `matches!(mode, BranchMode::Auto) && already_materialized && resume_allowed` and the verification text separately checks `BranchMode::Auto && already_materialized=false`. This mixes an unrelated `Create` mode into the acceptance criterion and conflicts with T012/AC-20.

T005 only adds `Bash` for `type-designer.md`, but `IN-09` and `AC-17` require `Write + Bash` for all three subagents and explicitly require `type-designer` to directly write `<layer>-types.json`. As written, this task cannot satisfy the acceptance criteria for subagent write permission.

`CN-10` says revised `track/*.md` bodies should contain only writer invocation and result receive, but `T007`/`AC-14` require a `TaskCreate` preamble in `/track:plan`. That preamble is extra command-body logic, so the plan contradicts its own command-slimming rule unless `CN-10` is relaxed first.

`AC-19` names `BranchMode::Create` as the false-path test condition, but `IN-10` and `T012` define the allowed true path as `BranchMode::Auto && already_materialized && resume_allowed`. The acceptance criterion is pointing at the wrong branch mode, so the required unit test is mis-specified.

CN-02 says each subagent may write only its corresponding SSoT file and touch nothing else, but the same spec requires those subagents to render generated view files: spec-designer renders spec.md, type-designer renders graph/contract-map/type-signal markdown, and impl-planner may render plan.md. As written, an implementation cannot satisfy both the writer-boundary constraint and the required subagent render pipeline; clarify that generated views are within the owning subagent's write scope or move rendering to another writer.

AC-13 includes init.md in the set of command definitions that must contain no schema validation/codec-round-trip procedure, but AC-01/CN-10/T001 explicitly require /track:init to perform identity schema binary verification via sotp verify track-metadata. This makes the /track:init acceptance criteria self-contradictory; AC-13 needs to exclude the /track:init identity check or AC-01/T001 need to move that validation out of the command definition.

spec.json reports `yellow: 0` even though many spec elements carry `informal_grounds`. The referenced ADR gate semantics make non-empty informal grounds yellow until they are formalized, so this artifact is claiming all 59 signals are blue while simultaneously relying on unresolved user_directive/memory grounds. That can hide the required ADR/convention formalization before merge.

CN-12 says each subagent writes only its corresponding SSoT file and nothing else, but CN-02/CN-13 and the in-scope requirements require those same subagents to render derived files such as `spec.md`, graph/type-signal views, `contract-map.md`, and `plan.md`. These are mutually exclusive write-boundary instructions for the agent definitions, so implementers cannot satisfy both constraints as written.

AC-13 explicitly applies to `.claude/commands/track/plan.md`, but task-coverage maps AC-13 only to T001-T004. The plan rewrite is done by T007/T008, so the coverage artifact can pass AC-13 without covering the command file that AC-13 names, leaving the forbidden-logic check for `plan.md` untracked.

The artifacts refer to OQ-6/OQ-7/OQ-8/OQ-10 as recorded open questions, and spec entries say differences are documented in those OQs, but the reviewed plan artifacts contain no open-question definitions or section for those labels. The OQ references are therefore not usable design records for reviewers or implementers.

AC-13 now says `/track:init` is explicitly excluded from the no-schema-validation/no-logic inspection because T001 is allowed to run `sotp verify track-metadata`, but the same rendered AC still maps AC-13 to `T001`. That leaves the plan internally inconsistent about whether `/track:init` must satisfy AC-13, and can make reviewers or implementers reject the required identity validation step.

CN-08 says every standalone phase command, including `/track:type-design`, must own its upstream artifact pre-check, but the same sentence only defines checks for `/track:spec-design` and `/track:impl-plan`; T004 and the T004 verification steps also omit any `spec.json` pre-check for `/track:type-design`. Since IN-06 marks `/track:type-design` as independently invokable, the plan leaves Phase 2 able to run without its required Phase 1 artifact despite the stated command contract.

AC-13 was revised to explicitly exclude `/track:init` because that command is the CN-10 writer=main exception, but the plan still presents T001 as satisfying AC-13: `impl-plan.json`/rendered `plan.md` list `AC-13` in T001, while `task-coverage.json` removes T001 from AC-13. That leaves the artifacts internally contradictory about whether `/track:init` must be checked under AC-13, so an implementer could apply the logic-free command-body constraint to the one command the spec now exempts.

AC-13 was changed to explicitly exclude /track:init because T001 is allowed to include identity schema validation, but the T001 verification checklist still says /track:init should be checked against AC-13. That makes the verification procedure contradict the updated acceptance criterion and can cause reviewers to apply the no-schema-logic gate to the one command the spec says is exempt.

task-coverage now claims AC-13 is covered by T008, but T008's task description does not list AC-13 among its covered criteria. Since AC-13 is the low-level-logic exclusion gate for plan.md and T008 adds the loop body to plan.md, the plan artifacts are inconsistent about whether T008 must verify that gate.

AC-13 is now assigned to T007/T008 for the rewritten `.claude/commands/track/plan.md` in the spec/task coverage, but the verification steps for T007/T008 only check the plan skeleton and loop behavior and never verify AC-13's forbidden schema/signal/reference/coverage/canonical-logic condition for `plan.md`. As written, the verification artifact no longer covers all tasks/ACs it claims to cover.

The new Open Questions section says it defines OQ labels that appear in the spec's `informal_grounds`, and it includes OQ-10, but OQ-10 is only defined in `verification.md` and is not recorded in `spec.json`/`spec.md` informal grounds. That makes OQ-10 invisible to the spec SSoT despite the verification artifact claiming it is part of the spec's informal grounds.

The spec still carries the promoted items as `informal_grounds.kind = "user_directive"` instead of moving them onto the new child ADR; `2026-04-22-0829` is not referenced anywhere in this file, so the claimed `blue: 59 / yellow: 0` state is not actually backed by the artifact contents. This starts at GO-05/GO-06 and repeats through the later IN/AC entries.

This ADR omits the repository-required `## Status` section, so the new index entry cannot be grounded in one of the documented lifecycle states (`Proposed` / `Accepted` / `Superseded` / `Deprecated`). That makes the README row fall back to an unlabeled `—` state.

The `signals` summary was changed to `blue: 59, yellow: 0`, but this same spec still contains unresolved `user_directive` informal grounds in multiple entries (for example GO-05, GO-06, IN-01, IN-02, and others). That makes the zero-yellow claim internally inconsistent with the file contents.

この ADR は `spec.json` の 31 件の yellow signal を本 ADR の `adr_refs[]` へ昇格させて解消したと説明していますが、現行の `spec.json` 側では該当項目がまだ親 ADR 参照のままで、本文で説明している評価ルールに従うとそれらはすでに Blue です。つまり『この ADR で yellow を解消した』という因果関係が、実際の artifact 群と噛み合っていません。

ADR D1 says the command definition body should describe steps (1)-(3), where step 3 is the post-write signal/binary CLI call, but the spec's CN-10/AC-22 require phase command definitions to contain only writer invocation plus result receipt and to contain no `sotp` CLI calls. This gives implementers contradictory guidance about whether CLI evaluation belongs in the command definition text.
